_schema.purpose: A per-screen CAPTURE CONTRACT: how to deterministically reach and capture one legacy screen so the evidence is real (usable, not just 'rendered'). The analyzer writes one of these per screen; capture_screen.py reads it with --profile; the BUILDER reuses the SAME profile to capture the React side, guaranteeing comparable captures.
rules: Prefer semantic readiness (waitFor + mustContain + waitForGone) over a big waitMs; waitMs is the LAST small settle, not the strategy.
Record the canonical route AND the misleading routes (e.g. a direct *.do that shows an error page).
workflow may be an inline list of steps (navigate/click/fill/select/wait) OR a path to a steps JSON.
Use the same profile (minus url) for the legacy and react captures.
recordHar:true records the REAL backend responses (responses.har) for record-mode replay (no fake data).
errorSignatures[] = text that marks a wrong/error page; a hit QUARANTINES the capture to _rejected/ instead of promoting it.
dataMode (record|live) tells the builder how this view's React side gets real data; capture itself ignores it.

examples.authContext: authenticated via the REAL login-form submit; FA context AB10. Reuse auth_state.json (--auth-state).
knownFailureModes: Opening dispatcherAction.do WITHOUT a live logged-in session shows the login/error route, not the summary.
Direct loginAction.do is a MISLEADING target (standalone error page) — never use it as a verification route.
FA-detail widgets hydrate ~8s after first HTML; capturing too early yields missing backend data.
examples.notes: Reach by submitting the real login form first (or reuse auth_state.json), THEN this dispatcher route. ~5s baseline + ~8s for FA-detail widgets.

examples.screen: Post-login FA Summary (via real form submit)
examples.canonicalRoute: loginAction.do (as a FORM POST, not a direct GET) -> dispatcher summary
examples.authContext: performs the real login, then lands on the authenticated summary
knownFailureModes: The app may try to recover auth via platform/GCS paths that don't exist on localhost — see runtime-readiness-and-auth.md.
examples.notes: Use this when you must prove the real login navigation rather than reusing a saved session.
